narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and a middle-class family background, you've put in the effort to pursue law school, and we appreciate your determination. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 41.6% chance of failure for you—high enough that it tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to protect the program's success rates. This prediction wasn't random; it weighed several key aspects of your profile against patterns from thousands of past students. Your undergraduate GPA stood out as the strongest positive factor, performing right around the typical 3.21 average and pushing the model toward seeing you as likely to pass, almost like an anchor of reliability in your academic record. Similarly, your middle-class family income aligned with common successful profiles, giving the prediction a helpful nudge in the favorable direction. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3—below the average of about 5.5—emerged as the top concern, strongly signaling to the model a heightened risk of bar exam struggles compared to higher-performing peers. Your LSAT score of 30, notably lower than the usual 36.7, added a smaller but still concerning push toward the failure prediction. On a brighter note, your choice of full-time study matched the overwhelming majority of students (over 92%) and provided some counterbalance by suggesting focus and commitment. Your Year 1 decile of 3 was a minor positive influence overall. Compared to typical admitted students, your GPA held up well, but the dips in LSAT, later-year deciles, and income bracket pulled you below the threshold where most passers cluster. This is tough news, but it's not the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year performance through targeted prep, or exploring part-time options if it fits your path better. Many students in similar spots have improved these areas and succeeded elsewhere; we encourage you to refine your profile and reapply stronger. Keep pushing forward—you've got the foundation to turn this around.
